description: Neurological emergencies for Resident Life simulator - stroke, seizures, hemorrhage, spinal cord compression

patients.name: Mr. Mahmoud
patients.complaint: Sudden onset right-sided weakness and inability to speak for 45 minutes. Wife witnessed at 08:15 while eating breakfast. Right face drooping, right arm and leg paralyzed. Global aphasia. BP 185/100, HR 88 irregular. Known atrial fibrillation, not on anticoagulation.
patients.triageNote: 67M, acute right hemiplegia + aphasia, onset 45 min ago, known AFib not anticoagulated, BP 185/100, NIHSS ~18
patients.correctDiagnosis: Acute Ischemic Stroke - Left MCA Territory (Likely Cardioembolic from Atrial Fibrillation)

result.finding: Blood glucose: 132 mg/dL
result.interpretation: Normal - hypoglycemia excluded as stroke mimic. Hyperglycemia >180 would be poor prognostic factor.

result.finding: No acute hemorrhage. No mass effect. Hyperdense left MCA sign (dot sign) in Sylvian fissure. Loss of gray-white differentiation in left insular cortex. ASPECTS score: 8/10 (early ischemic changes in lentiform nucleus and insula).
result.interpretation: Acute left MCA ischemic stroke. ASPECTS >7 = favorable for thrombolysis. Hyperdense MCA sign = thrombus in M1 segment. No hemorrhage = tPA candidate.

result.finding: Left M1 segment occlusion (proximal MCA). Good collateral circulation via leptomeningeal vessels. Right ICA and MCA patent. No carotid stenosis. No aortic arch disease.
result.interpretation: Large vessel occlusion (LVO) confirmed. Proximal M1 occlusion. Good collaterals = favorable for mechanical thrombectomy. tPA alone may not be sufficient for LVO.

result.finding: Core infarct: 15mL. Penumbra (tissue at risk): 85mL. Mismatch ratio: 6.7:1. Mismatch volume: 70mL.
result.interpretation: Large penumbra with small core = ideal for mechanical thrombectomy up to 24 hours (DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria). Patient within 4.5h window for tPA.

result.finding: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response 88-110. No ST changes. No LVH. Old non-specific T wave changes.
result.interpretation: AFib confirmed = likely cardioembolic source. No concurrent MI.

result.finding: WBC 8.2, Hb 14.5, Plt 245, INR 1.1, PTT 32
result.interpretation: Normal labs. No coagulopathy. Platelets adequate = tPA safe to administer.

result.finding: tPA bolus (10%) + infusion (90%) started at 09:05. Door-to-needle time: 50 minutes (target <60 min). Total dose: 67.5mg (patient weight 75kg). BP controlled to 175/95 with labetalol before tPA.
result.interpretation: tPA within 4.5 hours of symptom onset (onset 08:15, tPA 09:05 = 50 min). NINDS trial: 30% more likely to have minimal/no disability at 3 months. sICH risk: 6.4%.

result.finding: Thrombectomy performed via right femoral access. Left M1 thrombus retrieved with stent retriever (TICI 3 reperfusion). Onset-to-reperfusion: 180 minutes. Post-procedure: NIHSS improved from 18 to 6.
result.interpretation: Combined tPA + thrombectomy = best outcome for LVO. TICI 3 = complete reperfusion. DAWN trial: Thrombectomy up to 24h in selected patients.

result.finding: Pre-tPA: BP lowered to 175/95 with IV labetalol 10mg. Post-tPA: BP maintained <180/105 for 24 hours. Permissive hypertension allowed (up to 220/120 if no tPA).
result.interpretation: Aggressive BP lowering in acute stroke reduces cerebral perfusion. Pre-tPA target <185/110. Post-tPA target <180/105. Without tPA: only treat if >220/120.

result.finding: WARNING: Anticoagulation contraindicated within 24 hours of tPA administration. Delay DOAC/warfarin for 24h and until repeat CT confirms no hemorrhage.
result.interpretation: Starting anticoagulation immediately after tPA increases hemorrhagic transformation risk. Wait 24h + repeat CT.

treatmentPathway.wrongActionsConsequence: Anticoagulation within 24h of tPA = high risk of symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage

criticalErrors.within24hoursOfTPA: Absolute contraindication. Wait 24 hours + repeat CT negative for hemorrhage. Then start DOAC (apixaban 5mg BID) for AFib stroke prevention.

criticalErrors.consequence: Rapid BP drop >15% in first 24h worsens penumbra perfusion and increases infarct size. Maintain BP 140-180 systolic.

stages.event: NIHSS worsening. Right side completely plegic. GCS dropping.
Malignant MCA infarction. Midline shift developing.
Cerebral herniation. Coma. Unequal pupils.
Brain death from herniation.
tPA working. NIHSS improving 18 → 12.
Partial recanalization. But LVO may not fully open with tPA alone (only 30% recanalization rate for proximal MCA).
Residual deficit. NIHSS 8. Moderate disability likely.
Post-thrombectomy. TICI 3 flow. NIHSS 18 → 6.
NIHSS 4. Moving right side. Starting to speak.
NIHSS 2. Minimal deficit. Transfer to stroke unit. Excellent prognosis.

learningPoints.missedDiagnosis: If you thought it was hemorrhagic stroke: CT head non-contrast is mandatory and diagnostic. Hyperdense MCA sign = thrombus, not hemorrhage. Hemorrhage appears bright white on CT immediately.
learningPoints.keyTeaching: Door-to-needle time <60 min, door-to-puncture <90 min. tPA window: 4.5h from last known well. Thrombectomy window: 24h in selected patients (DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria). For AFib patients, check CHA2DS2-VASc score for long-term anticoagulation.
learningPoints.guideline: AHA/ASA Acute Stroke Guidelines: Non-contrast CT head STAT. If no hemorrhage and within 4.5h → tPA (0.9mg/kg, max 90mg). If LVO (ICA, M1, basilar) → mechanical thrombectomy. Permissive hypertension unless >220/120 or >185/110 if tPA candidate. No anticoagulation/antiplatelet for 24h post-tPA.

patients.name: Child Ali
patients.complaint: Active seizure for 8 minutes. Generalized tonic-clonic movements. Eyes rolled back. Foaming at mouth. Post-ictal confusion expected. First seizure. Mother reports fever 38.9°C for 2 days with runny nose.
patients.triageNote: 7M, active seizure ×8min, febrile 38.9°C, ?febrile seizure vs status epilepticus, HR 140, SpO2 96% on facemask O2

result.finding: Blood glucose: 118 mg/dL
result.interpretation: Normal for age. Hypoglycemia excluded as seizure cause.

result.finding: Lorazepam 1mg IV given (weight 22kg). Seizure stopped within 90 seconds. Patient now post-ictal, arousable but confused.
result.interpretation: Benzodiazepine = first-line for status epilepticus. Lorazepam preferred (longer duration, less respiratory depression). If no IV, use rectal diazepam 0.5mg/kg or buccal midazolam 0.3mg/kg.

result.finding: WARNING: Levetiracetam 40mg/kg IV indicated only if seizure persists >5 min after first benzo dose. This patient responded to benzo alone. Observe.
result.interpretation: Status epilepticus protocol: Step 1: Benzo ×1, repeat ×1 if ongoing at 5 min. Step 2: Levetiracetam 40mg/kg or Fosphenytoin 20mg/kg PE. Step 3: Anesthetic (propofol/midazolam) for refractory.

result.finding: Normal brain parenchyma. No mass, hemorrhage, or midline shift. No signs of increased ICP.
result.interpretation: CT normal - reassuring. However, MRI is preferred in children to avoid radiation. Consider MRI if atypical features.

result.finding: WBC 14.2, Neutrophils 72%, Hb 12.5, Plt 280, CRP 48 mg/L
result.interpretation: Mild leukocytosis and elevated CRP - consistent with viral URI. CRP <80 argues against serious bacterial infection.
All normal. Electrolyte abnormality excluded.

result.finding: WARNING: LP indicated only if suspicion for meningitis (neck stiffness, photophobia, Kernig/Brudzinski signs, petechiae, altered consciousness not returning to baseline). This child has typical febrile seizure with rapid return to baseline.
result.interpretation: Simple febrile seizure in child 6mo-5yr with fever, no CNS signs: LP not routinely indicated. Complex febrile seizure (focal, prolonged >15min, multiple in 24h): consider LP if <12 months or concerning exam.

result.finding: Acetaminophen 330mg given. Temperature decreased to 38.0°C. Child comfortable.
result.interpretation: Treating fever reduces discomfort but does NOT prevent febrile seizures (they occur with rapid temperature rise, not absolute height).

treatmentPathway.wrongActionsConsequence: Unnecessary LP is invasive and traumatic for child with typical febrile seizure. Second-line AED unnecessary if seizure stopped with benzo.

criticalErrors.whenNotIndicated: LP not indicated for simple febrile seizure in neurologically normal child >12 months with rapid return to baseline. Risks: post-dural headache, infection, spinal hematoma.

stages.event: Seizure continuing >15 minutes = established status epilepticus.
Hypoxia developing. SpO2 88%. Risk of neuronal injury.
Prolonged seizure >30 min = risk of permanent brain damage.
Seizure stopped. Post-ictal state. Child sleepy but arousable.
Returning to baseline. Recognizing mother.
Back to normal. Discharge with fever management education. Reassure parents: febrile seizures don't cause brain damage. Risk of epilepsy: 1-2%.

learningPoints.missedDiagnosis: If you thought it was meningitis: No neck stiffness, no photophobia, no petechiae, rapid return to baseline = typical febrile seizure. Meningitis presents with persistent altered mental status + meningismus.
learningPoints.keyTeaching: Simple febrile seizure: Generalized, <15 min, once per 24h, in child 6mo-5yr with fever, normal neuro exam post-ictal. Treatment: abort seizure if >5min (benzo), evaluate fever source. Reassure parents: benign, no long-term consequences, 30% recurrence risk. Antipyretics don't prevent seizures.
learningPoints.guideline: AAP Febrile Seizure Guideline: LP indicated if <12 months, meningeal signs, or not returned to baseline. EEG/MRI not routinely indicated. No prophylactic AEDs needed. Parental education is key.

patients.name: Mr. Samir
patients.complaint: Sudden severe headache 'like being hit with a baseball bat' (thunderclap headache). Worst headache of his life. Vomited twice. Neck stiffness. Photophobia. GCS 14 (confused). BP 195/110. No trauma history.
patients.triageNote: 55M, thunderclap headache, vomiting, neck stiffness, photophobia, confused, BP 195/110, ?subarachnoid hemorrhage

result.finding: Diffuse subarachnoid hemorrhage in basal cisterns, Sylvian fissures bilaterally, and interhemispheric fissure. Intraventricular extension in 3rd and 4th ventricles. Early hydrocephalus. Fisher Grade 4. No intraparenchymal hematoma.
result.interpretation: Diffuse SAH = likely posterior circulation aneurysm (basilar tip, PICA). Hydrocephalus = poor prognostic sign. Fisher 4 = high vasospasm risk.

result.finding: 5mm saccular aneurysm at anterior communicating artery (ACoA) with small bleb. No other aneurysms. Moderate vasospasm in bilateral ACAs.
result.interpretation: ACoA aneurysm = most common site (40%). Bleb = likely rupture point. Early vasospasm present (onset day 3-14, peak day 7-10).

result.finding: WARNING: CT is positive for SAH. LP unnecessary and potentially dangerous (increased ICP from hydrocephalus). LP only needed if CT negative but high clinical suspicion (sensitivity of CT decreases after 6h, 98% at 12h → 93% at 24h → 50% at 1 week).
result.interpretation: Do NOT perform LP with SAH on CT. Risk of herniation. LP reserved for CT-negative suspected SAH (looking for xanthochromia).

result.finding: Nimodipine started within 1 hour of diagnosis. Reduces vasospasm-related morbidity by 40%. Number needed to treat: 8 to prevent one poor outcome.
result.interpretation: Only intervention proven to improve outcomes in SAH. Must be given for 21 days. Mechanism: neuroprotection, not vasodilation per se.

result.finding: BP reduced to 140/85 with IV nicardipine infusion. Systolic maintained 120-140 (avoid hypotension to prevent cerebral ischemia). Pain controlled with morphine 2mg IV.
result.interpretation: Pre-aneurysm securing: Target SBP <160 (AHA) or <140 (ESO). After securing: allow higher pressures to maintain CPP. Avoid hypotension = risk of DCI (delayed cerebral ischemia).

result.finding: Neurosurgery activated. Plan: External ventricular drain (EVD) for hydrocephalus + Coiling vs Clipping within 24h. Coiling preferred for ACoA aneurysm (better outcomes vs clipping per ISAT trial).
result.interpretation: EVD needed for hydrocephalus. Aneurysm should be secured within 24h to prevent rebleeding (rebleed risk: 4% day 1, 1.5%/day for 2 weeks, 50% at 6 months untreated).

result.finding: WARNING: Tranexamic acid reduces rebleeding but increases DCI and does NOT improve overall outcomes. Not routinely recommended. Short course (<72h) if delay to aneurysm securing >24h.
result.interpretation: Controversial. Not standard of care in most centers. Focus on early aneurysm securing instead.

treatmentPathway.wrongActionsConsequence: LP with known SAH and hydrocephalus risks cerebral herniation

criticalErrors.whenSAHonCT: Absolute contraindication. Risk of herniation from CSF pressure gradient. LP only for CT-negative suspected SAH.

criticalErrors.consequence: Nimodipine is the ONLY proven neuroprotective agent. Must start immediately and continue for 21 days.

stages.event: Rebleeding! Aneurysm re-ruptured. GCS dropping to 6.
Massive intraventricular hemorrhage. Acute hydrocephalus.
Brainstem herniation. Death.
Nimodipine + BP control started. Awaiting aneurysm securing.
Vasospasm developing. TCD velocities rising. Risk of DCI.
Aneurysm not yet secured. Risk of rebleeding: 4% today, 15% by day 14.
EVD placed. ICP controlled. Nimodipine + BP control ongoing.
Coiling successful. Aneurysm secured. DSA confirms complete occlusion.
ICU monitoring for vasospasm day 3-14. TCD daily. Triple-H therapy if DCI develops. Nimodipine ×21 days.

learningPoints.missedDiagnosis: If you sent home as migraine: Thunderclap headache = SAH until proven otherwise. Sentinel headache (warning leak) occurs in 10-43% of SAH patients 2-8 weeks before major rupture. Always CT + LP if CT negative.
learningPoints.keyTeaching: SAH diagnosis: Non-contrast CT head (98% sensitive in first 12h). If CT negative → LP for xanthochromia (12h to 2 weeks). Management: Nimodipine ×21 days (only proven therapy), BP control (SBP <160), early aneurysm securing (<24-48h), EVD for hydrocephalus, TCD monitoring for vasospasm. Complications: Rebleeding (4% day 1), Vasospasm/DCI (peak day 7-10, 30% risk), Hydrocephalus, Seizures, Hyponatremia (SIADH/CSW).
learningPoints.guideline: AHA/ASA SAH Guidelines: Emergent CT non-contrast. If SAH confirmed → CTA/DSA to identify aneurysm. Nimodipine ×21 days (Class I). Early aneurysm treatment (<24h) via coiling (preferred for most) or clipping. EVD for acute hydrocephalus. TCD for vasospasm monitoring.

conceptsTested: Acute ischemic stroke: tPA and thrombectomy criteria
Subarachnoid hemorrhage: diagnosis and emergency management
